notes.anchor: the elections of the priestly colleges had, at my own utmost insistence, been put off to the next year
notes.note: The comitia sacerdotum were elections to Rome's major priestly colleges (pontifices, augurs, etc.); Cicero had lobbied successfully for postponement so that his son and the young republicans with Brutus could stand as candidates, preserving their honours even in absence.

notes.anchor: on Cicero's account, and on that of Domitius, Cato, Lentulus, and the Bibuli
notes.note: These are young aristocrats of the republican faction serving under Brutus: Cn. Domitius Ahenobarbus (future cos. 32 BC), the son of Cato Uticensis, a Lentulus Spinther, and the two sons of M. Calpurnius Bibulus (Caesar's colleague as consul 59 BC).

notes.anchor: the war has been reborn, and not slightly, through the crime of Lepidus
notes.note: After the battles of Forum Gallorum and Mutina in April 43 BC, Antony had appeared defeated; Lepidus's defection to him in late May revived the war and made a second round of fighting in Cisalpine Gaul or Narbonese Gaul likely.

notes.anchor: Caesar's army, which was the best
notes.note: Octavian's (the young Caesar's) veteran legions had fought loyally at Mutina but were now refusing to advance further against Antony, with whom Octavian was in secret negotiation; their inaction left Decimus Brutus and the Senate dangerously exposed.

notes.anchor: we have Brutus excellently joined with Plancus
notes.note: This refers to Decimus Brutus (not Marcus), who had escaped from besieged Mutina and was attempting to unite with Munatius Plancus's forces in Transalpine Gaul; "Brutus" here is unambiguously Decimus because Marcus Brutus is the recipient of this letter.

notes.anchor: on the Ides of March, when you drove out slavery from your fellow citizens
notes.note: The Ides of March (15 March 44 BC) was the date of Julius Caesar's assassination in the Theatre of Pompey by the Liberators led by Marcus Brutus and Cassius; Cicero uses it as the benchmark against which Brutus's coming service to Rome must be measured.
"V Idus Quintiles" = the fifth day before the Ides of Quinctilis = 11 July 43 BC; the month Quinctilis was renamed July in honour of Julius Caesar by the mid-40s BC.
